commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: Seventy. Some copies of the Septuagint add "five "with St. Stephen. See Genesis xlvi. 26. (Calmet)

commentaries.author: Gregory of Nyssa
commentaries.content: When we read in sacred history that Jacob went down into Egypt with seventyfive souls, we understand the flesh also to be intended together with the souls. So then the Word, when he became flesh, took with the flesh the whole of human nature. And hence it was possible that hunger and thirst, fear and dread, desire and sleep, tears and trouble of spirit, and all such things, were in him. For the Godhead, in its proper nature, admits no such affections, nor is the flesh by itself involved in them, if the soul is not affected coordinately with the body.

methodology.step_1: Parse main page with BeautifulSoup for structure
methodology.step_3: Use Firecrawl to get full content from 'Go to Commentary' links
methodology.step_4: Fallback to BeautifulSoup for accordion content if needed
advantages: Records verses even without commentaries for complete tracking
